## Vision and Mission Tangle Orchard Sutdios is a creative development studio aiming to create a profitable, sustainable company on top of open source development. The focus of our development is on Kestrel, a infinite-world adventure game based both in the heritage of tabeltop role-playing games as well as modern open world gaming. Another passion of the studio is _productivity_. We try to open source the tools (whether software or processes and habits!) as well on our journey to build Tangled Orchard. pages.string:
# Zero Config

**Work-in-progress**: collecting notes, but have not cleaned them up yet

I've wasted way too much time in my career trying to figure out idiosyncracies of different development tools, especially in the context of hobby projects where I'm trying to learn something else, not configure the perfect production system. As a result here are some conventions I use...

## Use make

It's certainly not perfect, but it's available on most systems and can be used as a common wrapper on other build systems so you don't have to remember what `cargo` versus `go` versus `rust` versus `npm` takes an argument.

Also, **use git**. Every assumption that can be made adds simplicity.

## Make targets

-   `ensure` - 
-   `build` -
-   `test` - 
-   `format` - aka 'lint', 'fmt'
-   `run` - 
-   `publish` - 
-   `dev` -
-   `benchmark` -
-   `doc` -

## In detail...

### `make dev`

**Intent**: automate as much of the build, display, testing, and results as possible for developing a particular project.

The `dev` target can be complicated as the what **specifically** is part of "automating as much as possible" is not clear and can lead to overly complex setups. On the other hand, small step that is necessary in every iteration of change that can be automated can go a long way. The complications in arriving at a standardized definition arise from the non-uniform nature of:

-   tasks at hand (scope)
-   development style

Is the **scope of the task at hand** to upgrade a dependent library and the core program to support new functionality?  If yes, it could be expediant to have that dependent library automatically rebuild and integrated.  But then does that mean all dependent libraries should be rebuilt just in case? Or maybe you're on a team and you are always working on library A and your teammate is the expert on libary B: how do you create a `dev` target that is optimal for you both?

The next consideration is **development style**. Perhaps you believe test-driven development starting with the library is the most efficient way to ensure upgrading the library and core program to have new functionality. Your teammate is a believer in "spiking" working functionality across the core program and library, then working backwards to generalize the API and code. One of those styles suggests running the unit tests on every change. The other suggests disabling testing until the core functionality is ready. I'm not sure there is an objective approach that is "always" better, so how does `make dev` allow for the right development setup?

## Common other targets

-   `build-watch` - watch files and rebuild locally. This can be useful for _other projects_ in a repo to invoke: i.e. watch all dependent projects (libraries, assets, etc.) so they are rebuilt automatically especailly in the context of a monorepo. This usually 

The purpose of this target is to start an  on-going process watch all source files and automatically trigger the `build` command when one of those files is modified. The work of this command is usually deferred to another program such as `nodemon`, `yolo`, or one of many other alternatives.  

This is usually a proper subset of the actions of `dev` target where the `dev` target may also run a development server, command-line processing, produce active output step after the automatic rebuild. The `dev` step also may invoke `build-watch` on dependent modules. `build-watch` should not watch dependent modules to avoid duplicated watches across shared dependencies.

## Some details

-   `.build-sentinel` - I have the `build` step `touch` a git-ignored file by this name whenever it is rebuilt. This makes chained "watching" of dependent projects simpler: the host project can kick of `build-watch` for all dependent projects and then it can watch the set of `.build-sentinel` files to know if and when it should rebuild itself.

## Things I wish were true...

#### A universal config/ sub-folder

Having so many .dot-files and config files in the root directory always distracts me. I've given up on this battle and some of my suggestions above contribute to the existing trends.

#### A standard JSON or AST format for documentation separating content from presentation

I wish Go, Rust, JavaScript, etc. all decoupled the data from the presentation!

## Context

I once wrote a program that examined projects and enforced many of these conventions (scanning .gitignore files, package files, adding eslint files, etc.). I decided documentation was easier to maintain that an automated approach.

## Reference

-   [GNU make standard targets](https://www.gnu.org/software/make/manual/html_node/Standard-Targets.html#Standard-Targets)

# Product Development through User Stories

_There are many approaches to product development, this is just one possible take on it..._

## Goal

The goal of this **user story centered** approach is to create a lightweight approach that both helps guide effective day-to-day work but also covers strategic, year-long planning in a connected way.  

## Overview

This approach puts **TODO-WIKILINK** at the forefront of what is generated and iterated on. These are collected into fixed size **epics** so a timeline can be formed to allow for stakeholders planning around the projects.  As these initially evolve a short **project charter** is created to set a long-term north-star for the team. These work together to create a balance of customer value deliverability, predictiability, and iteration on what is learned along the way.

This approach has a bias towards scope-reduction over schedule or resourcing adjustment (in the traditional scoped-schedule-resources balance of product development).

## Checklist

_Skipping "the why" here's a checklist_

-   Brainstorm "I want..." statements
-   Refinement
    -   Put statements into "As a {user role}, I want {goal} so that {why}."
    -   Split and combine user stories, group into Epics
    -   Add acceptance criteria
-   Move into Backlog

## Getting started

This section assumes you're starting from scratch on this process. After working in this fashion for a while, it becomes a more natural, iterative processs of improving on the prior work: we'll get to that in the next section.

Assuming you're working with great people, they're probably not excited about learning a new process, but rather getting start to doing higher-quality work: this section tries to speed start to the value and layer in the process that matters as it goes.

### Product Owner

### Generate a set of user stories

Expect the user stories to be varied and disorganized. The team is not yet aligned, so that's to be expected. Remember the following:

-   Anyone can write a story
-   The story should fit the format: _As a {user role}, I want to {goal or desire} so that {why}._
-   During brainstorming, do allow and record _I want to {goal}_: this can be refined later. Capture what people care about first.
-   These can be refined, grouped, and prioritized over time, but all are valid

### First pass of refining stories

Pair each with an **acceptance criteria**, which is a set of 1-3 unambiguous questions that, when all can be answered yes, the work can be considered done. This is also called the "definition of done."  This piece is critical to enable people to scope work back to the minimal viable solution (and thus meet a value-delivery timeline).

-   The acceptance critiera fit the format: _The {specific detail} must {detail}, so that {why this matters}._

Aside: functional stories are not often part of Agile process. The reason being they don't deliver direct customer value, which is supposed to be the entire intent of product development, right? Having worked as a product manager at a highly technical company (where much of the company value was based on some expert-class engineering), I found ignoring the functional stories can remove engineers excellent skill in identifying opportunities, economic advantages. This ignores a major value add.

Try not confuse user stories or functional stories with tasks. If you have tasks that feel exciting, write them down but hold off on bringing them up until the planning phase.

### Refining user stories

This isn't a discrete step, but rather should happen as naturally as possible as part of either the generation or review of user stories.  User stories should not aim for perfection, but do look for ways to improve them:

-   Is the goal focused on the user value? If not, can the story be rewritten in a way that expressed why the user cares about this?
-   Does the story emphasize how this needs to be implemented? If yes, can that be removed and still express the user value?  If yes _but there's a functional requirement that really matters embedded there_, move that to the acceptance critiera.

-   Does it have fuzzy words like "quickly" and "easily"? If yes, try to find ways to be more specific to avoid misaligned expectations and unclear goals.

## FAQ

### How do I keep the product cohesive? Everyone on my team has good ideas but they're all so different!

One approach - usually not best when first getting started - is bridging user stories before beginning work on them. Combine a succesffully completed user story and the new story:

_As a {user rule}, I use {product name} to {prior want} so {goal/why}.  I {new want} so that {goal/why}._

This can be helpful when existing user flows in the product may need attention due to their being incomplete, problematic for users, or there're opportunities to get more value from it. pages.string:
tags: how-to

# How to embed a bash script inside a Makefile

## Overview

Relies on Make's features:

-   [`define`](https://www.gnu.org/software/make/manual/html_node/Multi_002dLine.html)
-   [`export`](https://www.gnu.org/software/make/manual/html_node/Variables_002fRecursion.html)

The basic concept is this:

-   Use `define` to specify a multiline variable
-   Use `export` to ensure the multiline variable expands correct to the bash subshell

## Example

In this particular example, a bash script is used to wrap running multiple background 
processes together into logically a single Makefile command. 

```makefile
#
# Use a bash script since we want a single make command to run multiple
# background processes.
#
define DEV_SCRIPT

npx nodemon --watch data --ext js,jsx,mdx --exec make build-database &

npx webpack-dev-server \
		--mode development \
		--entry ./src/client.jsx \
		--history-api-fallback '/404.html' \
		--output-public-path dist \
		--output-filename client.bundle.js &
wait
endef
export DEV_SCRIPT

dev:
	@bash -c "$$DEV_SCRIPT"
```

## References

-   <https://stackoverflow.com/questions/7281395/output-multiline-variable-to-a-file-with-gnu-make>

pages.string:
# Principles

#### Routines

Follow from the "You must" principle. Make it a habit, so doing otherwise takes effort.

1.  Brainstorm who you would have yourself be
2.  Decide who you will be and simplify it to the most important aspects
3.  Determine what that person **must** do
4.  Teach yourself doing what you must is what brings you happiness
5.  Have to find joy in doing those things, not their results
6.  In every moment of your life, do everything you must immediately
7.  Relax, rest, and explore whenever that must be done is done

#### Problems

-   Want to do everything
-   Distractions by good things that are not most important
-   Improving without finishing (publishing, productionalizing, sharing, etc.)
-   Daydreaming
-   Waiting for tomorrow
-   Balance between being overly dramatic or not taking it seriously enough
-   Not taking pride in who you would have yourself be
-   Being tired, hungover, or in a bad mood
-   Being dragged away by friends or family
-   Being distracted by work
-   Not having the means, money, time, etc.

#### Reminders

-   It's okay to do the easier of the "musts" as long as it's a must

### Notes

-   Note about being embarassed of your apartment and inviting people
-   Meditation - didn't work for me. Exercise is when this works.
-   Podcasts - doesn't work during exercise for me, good for background
-   Themes - Own it, Train, Explore, Relax, ???
-   Digital Minimalism
-   Essentialism
-   What I use all the time (opposite of unboxings)
-   Making my bed - took a while, but realized it was one of at the cross of organization, just do it now
-   Clean your physical space to clean you mind
-   When unhappy try to give something away, throw something out, or just put it in storage

### Things

-   Anker SoundCore Headphone - need the over ear, sweat too much. Work reliably. Sound quality acceptable. Price not bad.
-   Garmin XT750

-   EDC (work, backpack)
-   Work Commute (dated) -

pages.string:
# Webpack Notes

## Goals, constraints, and background

-   Background
    -   I started with Deno to avoid Babel configuration, but how to build client React code was not clear to me
    -   Webpack complex-feeling configuration turned me to try parcel
    -   Parcel does not seem to do hot loading or impl
-   Goals
    -   Minimal configuration webpack development server

## Installation

```bash
npm i --save-dev webpack webpack-cli webpack-dev-server
npm i --save-dev babel-loader
```

```make
dev:
    npx webpack-dev-server
```

-   Using `npx` so global installs are not needed

## Babel configuration

In `webpack.config.js`:

```js
module.exports = {
    module: {
        rules: [
            {
                test: /\.(js|jsx)$/,
                exclude: /node_modules/,
                use: {
                    loader: "babel-loader"
                }
            }
        ]
    }
};
```

## Makefile configuration

-   Goals
    -   Want `nodemon` watching the database
    -   Want `webpack-dev-server` watching the source code
    -   Want to hide this in a single `make` command
-   Constraints
    -   Make doesn't seem to directly support running multiple processes in the background as part of the command

### Approach

-   Use `make`'s `define` command and `export` command to create a long-string
-   Encapsulate the bash script to run multiple watchers in that long-string

```make
#
# Use a bash script since we want a single make command to run multiple
# background processes.
#
define DEV_SCRIPT
shutdown() {
    echo kill $(jobs -l |awk '$2 == "|" {print $1; next} {print $2}')
    kill $(jobs -l |awk '$2 == "|" {print $1; next} {print $2}')
    exit 0
}
trap "shutdown" SIGINT SIGTERM

npx nodemon --watch data --ext js,jsx,mdx --exec make build-database &

npx webpack-dev-server \
		--mode development \
		--entry ./src/client.jsx \
		--output-filename client.bundle.js &
wait
endef
export DEV_SCRIPT

dev:
	bash -c "$$DEV_SCRIPT"
```

# Characters

## Contentment

Contentment is the "core" attribute of a character in Kestrel.

It is a measure of how well a character's expectations in are being met.  In some cases, this is can be thought of as a measurement of happiness, but not in all. A prisoner, enprisoned since childhood may find "contentment" that would still be a far cry from what might objectively be called happiness.  Thus, the character's expectations are play a key role in the determination of a character's contentment.

In general, a character's contentment is determined by:

-   The successes or failures of their [Strategies](strategy), [Plans](plan), and [Tasks](task)
-   Events and encounters that align to their [Values](value) and [Trigger](value)

### Fading

When a character's contentment stays negative for too many turns, they fade from the world. A

## Future Directions

In version 2.0, characters will also have lifetimes. Low contentment will create destructive behavior that is likely to reduce their lifetime, rather than directly fading.

# Occupations A character's **Occupation** is how the character "occupies their time." The character's occupation defines what how the character will act when left to their own devices. Every occupation is coupled to a some form of motivation (whether it's to pursue money, fame, excellence at their craft, defense of what they don't want to lose, etc.) and a strategy to up hold that motivation. The occupation of a character could be, but is not necessarily, their profession. Some example occupations might include: - **Farmer**: motivated to make a profit raising crops - **Trailblazer**: motivated to build trails into the unexplored - **Builder**: motivated to construct building in exchange for money from others - **Adventurer**: motivated to find treasure and defeat novel enemies - **Alcoholic**: motivated, perhaps unconsciously, to find more to drink - **Vagabond**: motivated to wander An occupation generally describes is how they spent their time, but each Occupation is coupled to a **Strategy** that defines what motivates the character in that activity and, therefore, how they approach it. ## Strategy A single Occupation can have multiple possible Strategies. Which Strategy a character chooses largely defines who they are. Let's take the example of the Farmer. Different Farmers might have different Strategies (and motiviations) in why they farm: **Example: A family farmer** A farmer, motiviated by the beauty of the field and to feed their family, has a strategy of cultivating small farms near their homes. They are not motivated to expand their fields once they have enough for their family, but instead will then try to improve the quality of what they are producing. The success of this Strategy will likely be determined by the health of their family as well as the amount of time the character can spend with their family. **Example: An ambitious business farmer** A farmer, motivated to run a successful business, has a strategy of expanding their fields and increasing how much produce they can sell over time. They are motiviated by financial success which in term makes them measure the quality of what they grow. These varied strategies may lead to very different outcomes. The different strategies also will have interesting effects when both are situated in the same area and other characters are looking to acquire the highest-quality food. Ultimately the success of the strategy affects the character's [contentment](contentment), which is the primary health indicator of a character. ## Plans A Strategy is a large, encompassing goal that drives the entire behavior of the character. In order to make that Strategy happen, each strategy is composed of different smaller **Plans** that the character undertakes. These are series of tasks. If some of the tasks fail, the plan may adapt or adjust or cancel. These are more complex. Plans are usually defined under the umbrella of particular Strategies (i.e. they are not reused across different strategies). ## Tasks These are "atomic" things a character can do in the world. They either succeed or fail. It's like digging a block. Tilling the soil for farming. Tasks are "reusable". Plans are composed as a sequence of Tasks. Task can have simple options to allow variations. They also may have a small set of different outcomes. Tasks can be Succeeded, Failed, Interrupted, or Canceled. A task may require certain Items or Skills to be undertaken. A Task can always be "forced" to be undertaken, but it will result in failure without the required Skills. Items, Skills, and Conditions may also affect the probability of Success. As Tasks are the building block of all Plans and Strategies, building a collection of high-quality Tasks ensure the world can support a robust set of behaviors. Last a task may be composed of different _Steps_. These are essentially sub-tasks which exist to help make it easier to develop new Plans in the gaming world. ## Future Directions **Multiple occupations**. Characters may have a profession as well as a hobby. They may have a daytime occupation as well as evening occupation. They may have multiple occupations with rules about which one decides their actions.

# Occupation: Farmer

## Strategy: Basic Farming

### Plan: Create the Plot

1.  Choose a NxM rectangular area for farming
2.  Level the terrain in that area
3.  Build a fence around the rectangle

If (1) is interrupted, reset the Task.  If (2) is interrupted, retry based on stubbornness, then reset the Plan.  

If (3) is interrupted, rety based on stubbornness. Possibly apply aggression if frustration is high.

If plan completes go to next plan...

### Plan: Till and Harvest

-   Till the soil in each square
-   Plant seeds in each tilled square
-   Wait for the planted seeds to grow
-   Harvest the plants
-   Repeat tilling the soil

pages.string:
# Occupation: Vagabond

The default occuptation.

`ˈvaɡəˌbänd` _noun_, a person who wanders from place to place without a home or job.

## Strategies

The Vagabond has exactly one strategy **Wander**. Any time a Vagabond lacks a strategy, she or he should begin to **Wander**.

### Wander

The Wander strategy alternates randomly between two Plans: Meander and Relax. The choice of plan does not consider any prior history or future goals, the Plan is determined via a d100 roll: 30 or less chooses Meander, otherwise Relax.

#### Meander

-   Choose a destination: a point of radius 10+d100 from the Character is chosen. If this spot is not a favorable destination, try again if d100 >= 10. A favorable destination is one with a path that puts the Character in not overt danger.  If interrupted, the Plan is canceled.
-   Roll a Reward of d100 > 5 of contentment, set a Loss of half the Reward.
-   Follow the path: A Travel Task is created with the path to the target.  This Task should allow for small interruptions and course corrections. If interrupted, d100 > 30 determines if the task is resumed or the plan abandoned,

On completion, the character gets the Reward,

#### Relax

At the start of this plan, a Duration is determined by a d100 roll. The Plan is then a single Task of to Wait for that Duration in their current spot. If the Wait is interrupted, the Plan is canceled and a new Plan should be chosen once the Character is no longer interrupted.

A Reward of d10 is available if completed. A Loss of d10 is interrupted.

## Also See

[Occupation](../occupation)

# Creating and Deploying a new Sea Site

## Quick Start

### Ensure sea is installed

Install the `sea` command-line program:

```bash
$ npm install --global @ridleywinters/sea
```

If you're not familiar with [`bash`](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bash_(Unix_shell)) or [`npm`](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Npm_(software)), some Google searching to get familiar with those tools would make sense first!

### Creating a starter project

Create a barebones example project (note: if you skip the `--template` argument, a bare-minimal project will be created):

```console
$ sea create myproject
```

You should now have a directory structure that looks something like this:

    myproject\
        components\
        pages\
            index.mdx

### Local testing

Now run the development server:

    $ sea dev

Lastly:

-   Visit http://localhost:8080!
-   Start modifying the MDX files to see the changes livee
-   Check out the [features](#features) section to play with some of the more interesting and unique features of Sea

## Make some basic changes

## Deploy

Note: this isn't sponsored! Neocities just has a nice, free, minimal setup that is a great fit given the goals here.  In the future, I'll aim to add documentation on how to deploy to GitHub Page, DigitalOcean, Amazon S3, and others.

children.value: Data Objects are a way to specify a common class of data independent of how it is displayed.

Let's say you want to use Sea to store or share a series of recipes. The recipes may have a common data format and you don't want to duplicate the style and presentation for every individual recipe (and spend a lot of time trying to keep those in sync if you want any changes).
You could use the following block to represent a recipe:

```@recipe
name: Spaghetti Dinner
ingredients:
    - spaghetti
    - water
    - pasta sauce
steps:
    - boil water
    - heat pasta sauce
    - put spaghetti in water for 8-10 minutes
    - drain spaghetti
    - put spaghetti in a bowl
    - pour pasta sauce on the spaghetti
    - serve
```

. Sea then parses the contents of the code section as YAML. This YAML then gets added to the Sea Tree as the parsed YAML data (rather than as a code block).
When the renderer comes across a Data Object it looks for a
with the same name as the type of the Data Object. In this case, that means it looks for a
React Component).  This Renderer will be passed the YAML data to render the Node.
An important use case to support is allowing individual field values to be parsed as Sea content.  Field names with a suffix of
are parsed as that type (unrecognized types are trimmed from the field name and ignored, per the principle that Sea should have reasonable default behavior for unavailable extensions):

pages.string:
# Data Objects

What are data objects?

Data Objects are a way to specify a common class of data independent of how it is displayed.

## Example

Let's say you want to use Sea to store or share a series of recipes. The recipes may have a common data format and you don't want to duplicate the style and presentation for every individual recipe (and spend a lot of time trying to keep those in sync if you want any changes).

You could use the following block to represent a recipe:

````yaml
```@recipe
name: Spaghetti Dinner
ingredients:
    - spaghetti
    - water
    - pasta sauce
steps:
    - boil water
    - heat pasta sauce
    - put spaghetti in water for 8-10 minutes
    - drain spaghetti
    - put spaghetti in a bowl
    - pour pasta sauce on the spaghetti
    - serve
```
````

#### Parsing

The `@recipe` label tells Sea to try the block that follows as a Data Object with the type `recipe`. Sea then parses the contents of the code section as YAML. This YAML then gets added to the Sea Tree as the parsed YAML data (rather than as a code block). 

#### Rendering

When the renderer comes across a Data Object it looks for a `Renderer` with the same name as the type of the Data Object. In this case, that means it looks for a `recipe.jsx` (or `Recipe` React Component).  This Renderer will be passed the YAML data to render the Node.

## Features

### Parsing fields as Sea content

An important use case to support is allowing individual field values to be parsed as Sea content.  Field names with a suffix of  `$[type]` are parsed as that type (unrecognized types are trimmed from the field name and ignored, per the principle that Sea should have reasonable default behavior for unavailable extensions):

    ```@two_columns
    columnA:  Hello **world**!
    columnB$sea:  Hello **world**!
    ```

In the above example, `columnA` will be parsed as plain text (the `**` will remain) and `columnB` will be parsed into an Sea Tree.

## Reference

### FAQ

```@expand
title: Why YAML? Aren't there cleaner, better options?
content$md: |
    YAML has its flaws, but is fairly common as a object-notation language.  It also is biased towards "ease of input" so is, arguably, a good match to the goals of Sea.

    Future versions of Sea may support other data object specification languages via custom parsers as Sea is intended to be extensible in data specification.
```

title: Sea
Work in progress page!
```@section style: marginTop: 64px textAlign: center content$md: | ![](sea_logo-v0.0.0.png) ```

Sea

## What is Sea? Sea is an **extension of Markdown** designed for **data-driven organization** that can be used to build dynamic web sites and knowledge. Sea has the **fluency of Markdown** combined with the data-driven extensibility of **React** and **YAML**. It is designed for **incremental, progressive improvement** from idea to production-quality work. Think of it as an **agile content and presentation** language. It intentionally tries to be as **minimal** as possible while acheiving these goals. There's one basic tool: ```bash $ sea build dirname/ --out database.json # builds database.json ``` Then to host a site all that is needed is to copy `database.json` into a folder with a a base `index.html` and `sea-site.js`. Site customization exists entirely in the database itself, so there's no need to modify the host html or script. Note: this is a _single page static website_ so free hosts like GitHub Pages can easily host this. No operational burden! For active development, to launch a local server with hot reloading: ```bash $ sea-build dev . ``` How do I customize it? If you know basic JavaScript and React, it should be very easy: Sea tries to inject as few "framework specific" concepts as possible. Note: Sea is more of a _technology_ than it is a _product_. It's very flexible and customizable. Sea templates can overlay an opinion on the technology, creating a clear, immediately usable use cases - thus making Sea into more of a product. Can be thought of as a programming language. ## What problems does it solve? Semantic wiki, TiddlyWiki, MediaWiki, Markdown, Gatsby, database, spreadsheets, etc. Another swing at a balance of that functionality. Incremental development of ideas. Allow things to be "finished" and published sooner yet continually improved. ## Quick Start Install the `sea` command-line program: ```bash $ npm install --global @ridleywinters/sea ``` Create a barebones example project (note: if you skip the `--template` argument, a bare-minimal project will be created): ```console $ sea create myproject --template=example ``` You should now have a directory structure that looks something like this: myproject\ pages\ home.mdx page1.mdx Now run the development server: $ sea dev Lastly: - Visit http://localhost:8080! - Start modifying the MDX files to see the changes livee - Check out the [features](#features) section to play with some of the more interesting and unique features of Sea ## How does it work? Sea operates on a directory of files, typically stored in git (for personal knowledge bases) or in a database for large multi-user use cases. Each file represents a **Card** - a sttarting point, think of that as a fairly standard Markdown document. Sea parses the whole set of Cards and creates a tree of data with indices so that a renderer can then easily render the documents as well cross-reference information in other Cards. ## Development Guidelines ### Keep it Minimal If something can be handled via a standard HTML, CSS, React, or Markdown, then push that functionality to the database. Provide documented examples and templates for that functionality rather than introduce it into the core. If the time to look up a recipe on how to add something to the data source is more or less equal to how long it would take to do it via the core functionality, then push it to the a plug-in. The challenge, of course, is to provide enough built-in conveniences that `sea` accelerates content creation and development, but not so many that the intracacies of `sea` itself becomes a distraction from that development. ### Simplicity over precision Make the rules of how sea works straightforward. Avoid exceptional cases where _ABC_ is true except under conditions _XYZ_, even if there's an argument that this more complicated rule is "cleaner". ## How does it compare? Sea is an extension of Markdown into multiple files to create a customizable database of information. It has the fluency of Markdown for simply writing text with minimal markup to support specifying data and formatting. It is customizable in that additional data types and renderers can be added to the database _directly_ as part of the database itself (no plug-ins, installation config, etc.). ```@expand title$md: How does it compare to [Notion](https://www.notion.so/)? content$md: | Leaner as it is command-line and file-based, it allows greater customization (it is easy to create custom "blocks"). However, it lacks the fluid UI, permissions model, and may other features that come out of the box with an app like Notion. ``` ```@expand title$md: How does it compare to [TiddlyWiki](https://tiddlywiki.com/)? content$md: | TODO! ``` ```@expand title$sea: How does it compare to [Evernote](https://evernote.com/)? content$md: | * Content is captured in files in git, giving full content and transparent on your data * No UI * No cross-platform syncing across multiple convenient apps * No features for quickly capturing data from other sources ``` ```@expand title$sea: How does it compare to [Obsidian](https://obsidian.md/)? content$md: | * I don't know! I have not tried Obsidian, but it seems similar! ``` ### Concepts - Data objects - Components (Renderers) - Parsers - Indices - Transformations (Graphs) - Exporters (Compilers) - Templates (?) - Style (?) Markdown with a couple extensions: - Data objects (YAML syntax) - Presentation objects (JSX syntax) Database: - All files parsed together and indexed for cross-referencing Server - Separate data from the presentation - API for operating on the data ## Publishing ### Publishing to GitHub Include an empty `.nojekyll` file in the root of your repository to [avoid having relative paths break](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/31611969/broken-image-paths-on-github-pages-without-jekyll)! ## Musings In one sense, Sea is a literal programming language for iteratively moving from idea to implementation. It's one that "starts" with plain text. Markdown is then layered in to a structure to the text. Data objects and transformations can then be added in to programmatically turn inputs into new outputs. Exporters can then make these transformations available in external tools (whether that's a binary asset, a code package, etc.). ## Roadmap #### v0.1 - Core - [ ] Create a command-line distributable with proper installation instructions - [ ] Create some basic documentation - [ ] Create a page to host it - [ ] Create a CHANGELOG #### v0.2 - Core - [ ] Ensure all core types can be overridden (consistentcy = simplicity) - Functionality - [ ] Embed pages - [ ] Show paths - [ ] Show tags - [ ] Respect `private` attribute - [ ] Make page directory accessible for parent/child relationships - [ ] Cookbook of common recipes #### v0.3 - [ ] Create a complementary web server #### v0.4 - Core - [ ] Create an API on the web server with edit commands - [ ] File write-back on API modifications - Usability - [ ] Add `sea init --template=` for multiple templates ### v0.5 - [ ] Create a flexible data source backend ### vX.X - [ ] Add templates - [ ] Add UI editors - [ ] Add a TableOfContents component - [ ] Rust / WASM support - [ ] Collaboration - [ ] Scale - [ ] Connecting pages into a book like mdbook ## Test Area ### Truly custom objects ```@section style: padding: '2px 8px' background: "rgba(20, 60, 200, 0.25)" content$md: | ### Hello world. * This is the beginning. * This is the middle. * This is the end. ``` ### Custom nodes The following creates a custom node in the AST of type `user_story`. The contents of the fenced section is parsed as YAML. ```@user_story story: As a user of this application, I want to specify custom objects in YAML so I can implement more visually appealing custom rendering. acceptance_criteria: - Must work - Must work well ``` By default, the renderer looks for a React renderer of the same name (in this case, `user_story.jsx`) and uses that to display the data object: ```@user_story story: As a user of this application, I want to specify custom objects in YAML so I can implement more visually appealing custom rendering. acceptance_criteria: - Must work - Must work well ``` ### Embedding specific data types This example uses the `$` suffix on a YAML field to indicate the parser that should be used on that field. In this case, it ensure the YAML field is parsed as Markdown (`$md`) so the `content` field is an AST as well. ```@expand title: This is an Expand object content$md: | This is a longer bit of content. I would prefer if this supported markdown and multiple lines, though. I need to consider some way to keep the AST going. * List Item A * Sub-item A1 * Sub-item A2 * List Item B * List Item C ``` ```@expand title: This is an Expand object content$md: | This is a longer bit of content. I would prefer if this supported markdown and multiple lines, though. I need to consider some way to keep the AST going. * List Item A * Sub-item A1 * Sub-item A2 * List Item B * List Item C ``` ## reference -
